_note: Home ports across North America, Caribbean and Latin America, with the regions reachable from each. 'lines' lists operators that commonly sail from that port — deployments change seasonally, so this is verified on a 60-day cycle. Never store fares here.
_source_guidance: Verify against each port authority's cruise schedule page and each line's published deployment.

hawaii.name: Hawaii
hawaii.note: Long sea-day crossings from the mainland

ports.name: Miami
ports.note: The busiest cruise port in the world — widest choice of ships and sailing lengths.

ports.name: Fort Lauderdale (Port Everglades)
ports.note: Strongest choice for longer and premium sailings, plus most Panama Canal departures.

ports.name: Port Canaveral (Orlando)
ports.note: Easiest pairing with an Orlando theme-park stay — heavily family-focused.

ports.name: Tampa
ports.note: Western Caribbean focus. Ships are size-limited by a bridge, so expect mid-size vessels.

ports.name: Jacksonville
ports.note: Small terminal, short sailings, very easy drive-up access.

ports.name: Palm Beach
ports.note: Short Bahamas getaways only — the lowest-commitment cruise in the country.

ports.name: Galveston
ports.note: The main Texas gateway — drive-to for much of the south-central US.

ports.name: New Orleans
ports.note: Pair the sailing with a few nights in the city — most travellers do.

ports.name: Mobile
ports.note: Compact terminal with short, value-focused itineraries.

ports.name: New York (Manhattan & Brooklyn)
ports.note: The only US port with scheduled transatlantic crossings.

ports.name: Cape Liberty (Bayonne, NJ)
ports.note: Serves the New York metro area with easier parking than Manhattan.

ports.name: Boston
ports.note: Prime autumn foliage departures in September and October.

ports.name: Baltimore
ports.note: Drive-to for the mid-Atlantic; sailings run longer to reach the Caribbean.

ports.name: Norfolk
ports.note: Seasonal schedule — fewer departures than the larger east coast ports.

ports.name: Charleston
ports.note: Limited seasonal programme; historic city worth arriving early for.

ports.name: Los Angeles (San Pedro)
ports.note: Main Pacific gateway for Mexico and Hawaii sailings.

ports.name: Long Beach
ports.note: Short 3–5 night Baja and Ensenada runs — popular weekend option.

ports.name: San Diego
ports.note: Relaxed terminal and a good base for a pre-cruise stay.

ports.name: San Francisco
ports.note: One of the few Alaska departures that avoids flying to the Pacific Northwest.

ports.name: Seattle
ports.note: The dominant US Alaska gateway — round-trip Inside Passage sailings.

ports.name: Vancouver
ports.note: Best for one-way Gulf of Alaska sailings that pair with a Denali land tour.

ports.name: Seward / Whittier
ports.note: Northern end of one-way Alaska sailings — usually combined with a cruisetour.

ports.name: Montreal
ports.note: St. Lawrence departures, strongest in the autumn foliage season.

ports.name: Quebec City
ports.note: Scenic river departure; the old town is worth an extra night.

ports.name: Halifax
ports.note: More commonly a port of call than a home port — check season.

ports.name: San Juan
ports.note: Sailing from within the Caribbean means more islands and less sea time.

ports.name: Bridgetown
ports.note: Deep Southern Caribbean itineraries reaching islands the big ports can't.

ports.name: Philipsburg
ports.note: Mostly small-ship departures — check which seasons operate.

ports.name: Cartagena
ports.note: Usually a port of call; home-port sailings are limited and seasonal.

ports.name: Colón
ports.note: Gateway for partial Canal transits and Central American itineraries.

ports.name: Buenos Aires
ports.note: Southern-summer season only, roughly November to March.

ports.name: Ushuaia
ports.note: The world's main Antarctica departure point — expedition ships only.

ports.name: Valparaíso / San Antonio (Santiago)
ports.note: Pairs with Patagonia and the Chilean fjords.

ports.name: Rio de Janeiro / Santos
ports.note: Brazilian coastal season runs during the southern summer.

ports.name: Callao (Lima)
ports.note: Usually a port of call on longer South America itineraries.

ports.name: Baltra / San Cristóbal
ports.note: Small expedition ships on fixed government-regulated itineraries.

ports.name: Ensenada
ports.note: Predominantly a port of call rather than a home port.
